blurb: A fast trailing mat of round golden leaves — chartreuse in shade, brassy gold in sun — spangled with bright yellow cup flowers in summer; a moisture-lover that will run, so site it with care.
sun: Part shade to full sun (gold in sun, chartreuse in shade; may scorch in hot afternoon sun)
water: Moderate to high — keep consistently moist; tolerates wet ground, not for dry spots
spread: Runs by rooting stems; can be aggressive — contain it
seasons: Golden-to-chartreuse round leaves spring through fall (semi-evergreen in mild spells); bright yellow cup flowers in summer
wildlife: Yellow summer flowers offer some pollen and nectar; grown mainly for its glowing foliage
deer: Not documented as a significant deer or rabbit target
habitat: Wet meadows, stream & pond banks, damp woodland

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy — USDA zones 3–9 — so it overwinters easily in Boulder's 5b–6a; the golden 'Aurea' form is a touch less vigorous than the plain green species.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after frost or in fall, in consistently moist ground or as a spiller in a well-watered pot; in our dry air and alkaline clay it needs steady moisture and some relief from hot afternoon sun to keep the foliage from scorching.[1][2]
care.sun: Leaf color is light-driven: brassy gold in full sun (morning sun is ideal), softening to lime-chartreuse in shade. Under intense high-altitude Front-Range sun give it afternoon shade or extra water to prevent sun scald.[2]
care.soil: Unfussy about soil type or pH — clay, loam or sand, acid to alkaline — as long as it stays moist; it will even grow in the shallow margins of a pond.[1][2]
care.water: A genuine moisture-lover: keep it damp. It thrives where other groundcovers would rot and tolerates boggy ground and standing water, so it is the wrong plant for a dry, unirrigated bed here.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space 12–18 in apart; it closes the gaps fast as the stems root down at every node.[1]
care.propagation: Trivially easy by division or cuttings — lift rooted chunks from the edge of a patch, or lay a stem on moist soil and it roots at the nodes. In North America it sets little or no viable seed, so it moves only vegetatively.[1][2]
care.bloom: Bright yellow, five-petaled cup flowers open along the stems through summer; the golden form blooms less freely than the green species, sometimes making leafy mats with few flowers.[2]
care.maintenance: Almost no care beyond keeping it watered and in bounds. Its running, node-rooting stems spread quickly — the green species is invasive in wet natural areas and even this cultivar can run — so edge it, pull stray stems, and never plant it where it can escape to a creek or wetland. Its shallow roots make stray growth easy to pull.[1][3]
care.troubles: Trouble-free apart from leaf scorch in dry, hot sun and its own tendency to wander; correct siting and steady moisture solve both.[2]

references.name: NC State Extension Plant Toolbox — Lysimachia nummularia
UW–Madison Extension (Wisconsin Horticulture) — Golden Creeping Jenny
USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species — Lysimachia nummularia

edible.summary: Not a food plant — creeping Jenny is grown purely as ornamental groundcover and has no established culinary use.[1]
edible.caution: Grown for its foliage, not for eating; no part is recommended as food and there is no recommended preparation. It is not considered dangerously poisonous, but keep it ornamental.[1][2]

gaps: Photos are species/type-representative Lysimachia nummularia — the golden 'Aurea' foliage is shown where a CC image exists; some flower and mat shots are of the green species form, which is identical but for leaf colour.
